meta.title: X351 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every Jaguar XJ X351 engine — 3.0 V6 diesel, 5.0 V8, XJR supercharged. Real UK costs and honest reliability data. Get free quotes from 15+ vetted Jaguar specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a Jaguar XJ X351 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned X351 engine typically costs £2,500–£10,000 supply only [JAG-QUOTE], depending on the engine — the 3.0 V6 diesel sits at £4,500–£7,000, the 5.0 V8 at £4,000–£6,500, and the supercharged XJR at £5,500–£10,000. Fitting adds £650–£1,170. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

mainEntity.name: Which X351 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The 3.0-litre V6 diesel (AJV6D) is the X351's most dependable engine — documented examples exceeding 200,000 km without major issues. The 3.0-litre supercharged V6 (306PS) is also robust, though cooling system pipework and supercharger insulator springs need attention. The 5.0 naturally aspirated V8 is reliable but watch for early timing chain guide wear on pre-2013 cars.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Jaguar XJ X351?

acceptedAnswer.text: Early 5.0-litre AJ133 engines (pre-2013) suffer from timing chain guide wear — an engine-out job costing £2,500–£4,500. PCV diaphragm failure on 5.0 V8s causes oil consumption and lean running codes. The 3.0 supercharged V6 has cooling system pipework leaks and supercharger insulator spring issues. Battery-related electrical gremlins and sticky buttons (rubberised coating breakdown) affect all models.

mainEntity.name: Is the Jaguar XJ X351 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends on the engine and year. The 3.0 V6 diesel is genuinely robust — many examples exceed 150,000 miles. The 5.0-litre AJ133 is generally reliable but pre-2013 examples need timing chain work. The supercharged variants add complexity — PCV diaphragms, vacuum pump oil leaks, and cooling system issues are common. Regular maintenance is critical; these are high-performance, high-maintenance flagships.

mainEntity.name: Which X351 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: Pre-2013 5.0-litre supercharged AJ133 (XJR, Supersport) without documented timing chain work — the aluminium guide wear issue is well-documented and expensive to fix. A 2.0 i4 Turbo without clear service history is also high-risk, primarily due to parts scarcity in the UK. Avoid any engine without full service history.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my X351 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Timing chain rattle on cold start (5.0 AJ133), persistent oil consumption or lean running codes (5.0 PCV diaphragm failure), coolant loss or overheating (3.0 V6 SC), persistent warning lights caused by low battery voltage, or sticky buttons and sunroof corrosion are the key warning signs. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing an X351 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the car's value. Against a £20,000 XJR, a £5,500–£9,000 recon is 28–45% of the car's value — well within our 60% Jaguar threshold. Against a £10,000 3.0 V6 diesel, a £4,500–£7,000 recon is 45–70% — borderline but often viable for a well-kept example. For a £15,000 5.0 naturally aspirated, a £4,000–£6,500 recon is 27–43% — a repair decision, not a scrap question. Value the car and its condition first; if it's a pre-2013 5.0 without chain history, walk away or budget for the work immediately.

mainEntity.name: How long do X351 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The 3.0 V6 diesel can exceed 200,000 km with proper servicing. The 5.0-litre V8 is capable of high mileage but timing chain guides are the limiting factor on early cars — addressed proactively, it can comfortably reach 120,000+ miles. The 3.0 supercharged V6 is robust but cooling system maintenance is critical. The 2.0 i4 Turbo is less documented, but parts availability may limit longevity in the UK.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the X351, X350, and other XJ generations?

acceptedAnswer.text: The X351 (2010–2019) is the fourth-generation XJ — it featured an all-aluminium architecture and Ian Callum's dramatic new design language. It's fundamentally different from the X350 (2003–2009), which had a steel chassis and more traditional styling. The X350 is covered on its own Generation Page. The X351 was the last traditionally-powered XJ; it was succeeded by the all-electric XJ which was cancelled in 2021.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my X351 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation; the engine code is also stamped on the cylinder block. For the diesel, look for AJV6D (3.0 V6). For petrol, it's AJV6 (3.0 naturally aspirated), AJV6 / 306PS (3.0 supercharged), 2.0T (2.0 i4 Turbo), AJ133 / 508PN (5.0 naturally aspirated), or AJ133 / 508PS (5.0 supercharged). Tell us your variant and registration year and we'll identify it — 15+ vetted Jaguar specialists will confirm it for free.

hero.h1: Jaguar XJ X351 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the X351 flagship saloon — from the 3.0-litre V6 diesel and supercharged V6 to the 5.0-litre naturally aspirated and supercharged V8s. Real UK replacement costs and honest reliability data for Jaguar's last traditionally-powered XJ. Backed by 151+ real UK enquiries in 2025 [JAG-VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: Jaguar XJ X351 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the X351 specifically — including the 3.0 V6 diesel, the 3.0 supercharged V6 that replaced it, the 2.0 turbo four for the Chinese market, and the full range of 5.0 V8s.

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: All ratings are provisional — no dedicated Engine Page exists for any X351 engine family. The 3.0 V6 diesel (AJV6D) is widely regarded as the most dependable, with documented examples exceeding 200,000 km without major issues. The 3.0 supercharged V6 (306PS) is also robust, though cooling system pipework and supercharger insulator springs can fail. The 5.0 V8 naturally aspirated (508PN) is generally reliable but watch for timing chain guide wear on earlier examples. The 5.0 supercharged V8s (508PS) add complexity — PCV diaphragms, vacuum pump oil leaks, and cooling system issues are common. The 2.0 i4 turbo is the least common and primarily served the Chinese market; its reliability is less documented.

overview.intro: The X351 is the fourth generation of Jaguar's flagship XJ saloon, launched in 2010 as the first model to feature Ian Callum's new design language. Built on a revolutionary all‑aluminium architecture — claimed to be the lightest in its class by 150 kg — it introduced a dramatic, coupé‑like silhouette that transformed the XJ's image. The engine line‑up evolved significantly: at launch, the XJ offered a 3.0‑litre V6 diesel, a 385 PS 5.0‑litre naturally aspirated V8, and a 510 PS 5.0‑litre supercharged V8. In 2012, a 3.0‑litre supercharged V6 and a 2.0‑litre turbocharged four‑cylinder were added for the growing Chinese market. The flagship XJR arrived in 2013 with a 550 PS supercharged V8. Production ended in July 2019 after more than 120,000 units.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: Jaguar X351 (all‑aluminium monocoque, front‑engine, rear‑wheel drive) • Predecessor: Jaguar XJ (X350) • Successor: None (XJ was discontinued; no direct successor) • Sibling body styles in this generation: 4‑door saloon (standard and long wheelbase)
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The XJ X351 generated 151 real UK engine enquiries in 2025 [JAG-VERIFIED], with the 3.0 V6 diesel and 5.0 V8 supercharged variants accounting for the majority of demand.

items.quote: The 3.0-litre supercharged V6 offers 340 PS of smooth, responsive power without the V8's running costs or complexity. Cooling system pipework and supercharger insulator springs are the only real watch points. It's the sweet spot in the petrol range.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want modern petrol refinement without V8 running costs.

items.quote: Timing chain guide wear on early 5.0-litre AJ133 engines is a known issue. PCV diaphragms rip with age, causing oil consumption and lean running codes. Vacuum pump oil leaks are common. Later cars received updated tensioners.
items.whoItsFor: buyers with documented timing chain work or post‑2013 cars.

items.quote: Timing chain replacement on the 5.0-litre AJ133 is an engine‑out job requiring specialist tooling — £2,500–£4,500 is typical. A full engine replacement can exceed £9,000. The XJR's 550 PS variant adds parts scarcity and cost.
items.whoItsFor: buyers with deep pockets who want the ultimate XJ flagship.

items.quote: The 385 PS 5.0-litre naturally aspirated V8 delivers V8 grunt without the supercharger's additional complexity. It's cheaper to buy, run, and repair than the XJR. Watch for timing chain guides on early cars, but otherwise robust.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want V8 presence without supercharger costs.

items.quote: The 238 PS 3.0-litre naturally aspirated V6 was the entry-level petrol before the supercharged V6 arrived in 2012. It's smooth and refined but underpowered for the XJ's size. Rare in the UK, but a good choice for those who prioritise simplicity over pace.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want V6 refinement without forced induction.

rows.commonMajorFailure: Timing chain guides, PCV, vacuum pump
rows.ownershipVerdict: High Power, High Cost — budget for maintenance

economicsRule.title: The X351 rule
economicsRule.text: A £5,500–£9,000 5.0 XJR recon against a £12,000–£25,000 XJR sits at 22–75% of the car's value — at the upper end, that brushes our 60% Jaguar scrap threshold. Against a £20,000+ example, the same recon drops to 28–45% — a repair decision, not a scrap question. For a 3.0 V6 diesel at £10,000–£18,000, a £4,500–£7,000 recon is 25–70% — borderline on lower‑value examples. Value the car first; if it's a pre‑2013 5.0 without timing chain history, walk away or budget for the work immediately.

keyTakeaways.question: Which X351 Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: 3.0 V6 Diesel (AJV6D) — proven reliability, reasonable economy, no supercharger complexity.

keyTakeaways.question: Which X351 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: 5.0 XJR (550 PS) — timing chain work is engine‑out, parts are scarce, and running costs are eye‑watering.

keyTakeaways.question: Which X351 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: 5.0 V8 Naturally Aspirated (508PN) — V8 presence without the supercharger's costs or complexity.

problems.title: 5.0 AJ133 Timing Chain Guide Wear
problems.recommendation: If you're buying a pre‑2013 5.0-litre X351, look for documented timing chain work or budget £2,500–£4,500 immediately. A preventative chain and tensioner replacement is dramatically cheaper than the £5,500+ replacement that follows a failure. On a car worth under £15,000, evaluate carefully before committing.

problems.title: 5.0 AJ133 PCV Diaphragm Failure
problems.recommendation: If your 5.0 V8 develops oil consumption or lean running codes, have the PCV diaphragms inspected immediately — a £400–£900 repair is far cheaper than the £4,500+ replacement that follows a catastrophic failure. On supercharged cars, it's worth combining this with timing chain work as the supercharger is already off.

problems.title: 3.0 V6 Supercharged (306PS) Cooling System
problems.recommendation: Have the cooling system inspected at every service — a £400–£800 preventative repair is far cheaper than the £4,500+ replacement that follows an overheat. If you hear a knocking noise from the front of the engine, have the supercharger insulator spring checked immediately.

problems.title: 2.0 i4 Turbo (Chinese Market)
problems.recommendation: This engine is rare in the UK. If you're considering a 2.0T X351, ensure parts availability and specialist support exist locally. A £500–£1,200 turbo repair is manageable, but a full replacement at £2,500+ may not be justified given the car's value.

problems.title: Battery & Electrical Gremlins (All Models)
problems.recommendation: Before chasing electrical faults on an X351, check the battery voltage first. A weak battery (below 12.4V) can cause a cascade of warning lights that disappear with a new battery. If you're buying an X351, budget for a new battery immediately. For early cars, check the sunroof runners for corrosion.

replacementCosts.h2: XJ X351 Engine Replacement Costs — By Engine and Condition

coreVariants.scopeNote: All variants listed are X351 only. The 2.0T was primarily offered in the Chinese market but is also present in limited numbers in the UK. The XJR (550 PS) arrived in 2013, with the XJR 575 and XJ50 special editions launched in 2018.
